summary:
Niah Cave is a large cave system in the Niah National Park of Sarawak, Malaysian Borneo, with one of the longest archaeological sequences in Southeast Asia, spanning from approximately 46,000 BCE to recent times. The site is famous for the discovery of a modern human skull — the Deep Skull — dated to approximately 37,000–40,000 BCE, making it one of the oldest anatomically modern human remains from island Southeast Asia and evidence of early human occupation of Borneo.

For the period 10,000–8,000 BCE, Niah Cave was occupied by Hoabinhian hunter-gatherers who exploited the rich forest and cave resources of the interior of Borneo. The faunal assemblage from this period includes pigs, deer, bearded pigs, porcupines, and a range of other forest mammals, along with fish and freshwater mollusks. The rich organic preservation in the cave deposits, combined with its extraordinary depth of occupation, makes Niah Cave an invaluable archive of environmental and cultural change in island Southeast Asia across tens of thousands of years, from the period when Borneo was connected to mainland Asia via [[event:1318|Sundaland]] through the flooding of the land bridges and into the Holocene. Niah's cultural sequence documents the persistence and adaptability of foraging communities in Borneo's equatorial forest environment.
